Tafseer:
Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(O Prophet, why do you prohibit [yourselves from] what Allah has made lawful for you, seeking the approval of your wives? And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful) (1).
(Accountability regarding an action of the Prophet [peace and blessings of Allah be upon him]): As noted above, this Surah teaches that in matters of Allah's limits and rights, no compromise or partiality is permissible, even with those with whom one shares relations of love and kindness. Rather, the very requirement of this love is that their accountability be conducted with greater care, so that, if possible, their reform can be achieved before being sifted through God's sieve; and if reform is not achieved, then at least one is absolved of the duty of counseling before Allah. To demonstrate this truth, Allah Almighty first corrected His Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in this Surah, questioning why he prohibited for himself something that Allah had made lawful for him, merely to please his wives.
Consider that who could be more beloved to Allah than His Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his wives? Yet, despite this, he was corrected for a minor lapse which, according to the Qur'an itself, was motivated by a highly noble intention. This was done so that it becomes entirely clear to everyone that if the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his wives are not above observing Allah's limits, then what can be said of others!
(The nature of the incident): As for the question of what the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) had prohibited for himself to please his wives, various reports have been narrated by the transmitters. The most famous narration is that he once consumed honey at the home of one of his wives, and some of his other wives expressed dislike for its smell. Indeed, some types of honey have an unpleasant odor, and even if they do not, those who possess highly refined senses do not like every smell. Women, in particular, are highly sensitive in this regard, sometimes disliking even perfectly fine and tasty things. People have different temperaments. Some of the Mothers of the Believers did not like the honey that had the smell of maghafeer (a specific herb from which bees gather nectar, but its smell is unpleasant to some). Thus, they expressed their dislike to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Because the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself was of extremely refined temperament and highly considerate of the feelings and emotions of others—especially the vulnerable gender—he resolved never to consume honey again.
Under normal circumstances, this was not unusual; rather, being motivated by a noble intention, it would be highly commendable. However, every word and deed of a prophet holds great weight in religion, and his every action serves as a model for the entire Ummah. Therefore, it is not permissible for him, even for his personal taste or to please his most beloved people, to say or do anything that exceeds the limits prescribed by Allah even by a hair's breadth, lest a wrong precedent be set for the entire Ummah. It is well known regarding the Jews that they prohibited camel meat for themselves merely because Prophet Jacob (peace be upon him) did not eat it for some reason. Similarly, if Muslims came to know that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) had vowed not to eat honey, any God-fearing Muslim would have hesitated to touch honey. For this reason, Allah Almighty corrected him and immediately directed its rectification.
(The motive of the Prophet's action): (seeking the approval of your wives) - This points to the motive that led to his action. Pointing out this motive was necessary because the purpose of this Surah, as stated in the introduction, is to teach that even when relations of love are involved with the truth, one must not be compromising in their accountability. Like hatred, love can also make a person heedless of respecting the divine limits. To warn against this danger, Allah Almighty corrected His Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his wives—who were more beloved in the sight of Allah than anyone else—so that Muslims might learn the lesson that in matters of religion, no consideration is permissible even for the most beloved of people.
(Correction of the error accompanied by the declaration of forgiveness): Since the motive of the Prophet's action was noble—he had made a personal decision out of kindness and affection, with no intention of prohibiting anything for the Ummah—Allah Almighty accompanied the correction with the declaration of forgiveness for this lapse. It is worth noting here that the declaration of forgiveness was made with great promptness, even before explaining the Shariah ruling for such an action. The reason for this is obviously that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) had no intention whatsoever of doing what he was corrected for; rather, as noted above, he had merely imposed a restriction on himself out of kindness to please the vulnerable gender. Thus, Allah corrected him to prevent the potential harms that could arise, but accompanied it with the declaration of forgiveness so that the correction would not weigh heavily on the Prophet's heart.
(The nature of lapses committed by prophets): Remember what we have repeatedly explained in this book: when prophets commit a lapse, it is not out of self-interest, but rather because they exceed the desired limit in pursuing some good. Pleasing one's wives is not a bad thing; indeed, it is a requirement of nobility, kindness, and chivalry, and is demanded by nature and the Shariah, which the Qur'an has emphasized—provided it remains within the limits of the Shariah. If it begins to exceed those limits, it becomes a fitnah (trial) from which it is necessary to guard oneself and others. However, when the motive behind a lapse is noble, the correction should be presented in a manner where pardon and forgiveness accompany it.

(Allah has already ordained for you the dissolution of your oaths. And Allah is your protector, and He is the Knowing, the Wise) (2).
(The Shariah's ruling regarding impermissible oaths): This states the ruling for when someone swears an oath to prohibit for themselves something that is lawful. In the preceding verse, the address was solely to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), but in this verse, it is to the Muslims in general. This is because correcting the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) for this lapse was primarily intended to prevent the Ummah from falling into any misunderstanding. Therefore, in this verse, all Muslims are addressed and informed that if any of them swears an oath to prohibit a lawful thing for themselves, Allah has made it mandatory for them to break that oath and make the prohibited thing lawful again.
(And Allah is your protector): This explains why Allah has made this necessary. It is stated: Allah alone is your Master and Protector, and therefore He alone has the right to determine what is lawful and what is unlawful for you. No one has the right to declare anything lawful or unlawful on their own, nor is it permissible to recognize such a right for anyone else. If someone does so, they interfere with Allah's exclusive right, which is a form of shirk.
(and He is the Knowing, the Wise): This further explains that the true Knower and Wise is Allah alone, and therefore whatever He has commanded His servants or prohibited them from is based entirely on knowledge and wisdom. No one should suffer from the delusion of being more knowledgeable or wiser than God. A question arises here: breaking the oath has been commanded, but no expiation (kaffarah) is mentioned alongside it; does this mean no expiation is required? The answer is: the ruling for the expiation of oaths was already explained in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 89, so there was no need to repeat it here. However, the words 'the dissolution of your oaths' (tahillata aymanikum) clearly indicate that if someone prohibited something through an oath, expiation is mandatory; but if no oath was sworn, no expiation is required.

(And [remember] when the Prophet confided to one of his wives a statement; and when she informed [another] of it and Allah showed it to him, he made known part of it and avoided part. And when he informed her of it, she said, 'Who informed you of this?' He said, 'I was informed by the Knowing, the Acquainted') (3).
(An example of the accountability of the Prophet's wives): In the preceding verse, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was held accountable. In this verse, one of his wives is corrected for a lapse: she disclosed a statement to another wife which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) had confided to her as a secret. When the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) corrected her for this mistake, instead of feeling remorseful, she felt corrected in her pride, and the wife to whom the secret was disclosed also felt displeased. Ultimately, Allah Almighty warned both wives so that it becomes clear to everyone that in matters of religion, even the wives of the Prophet are not above accountability.
(The nature of the incident): (And when the Prophet confided to one of his wives a statement) - Commentators have generally linked this incident to the preceding one, turning it into an unpleasant narrative. However, from the perspective of Arabic grammar, it is not necessary for this incident to be a part of the preceding one; rather, it is more likely a reference to another independent matter involving one of the Prophet's wives, for which Allah corrected her. Note that the word 'wa idh' (And when) is generally used to introduce an independent, separate event.
Here, the Qur'an does not clarify what the secret statement was or to which wife it was spoken; instead, it has kept it under a veil. Therefore, we do not consider it permissible to pry into this secret. The wives of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) hold the status of mothers for us, and it is in no way appropriate for children to pry into the secrets between their parents—especially when disclosing the secret does not assist in understanding the verse. Here, the warning is against disclosing the secret itself. If we pry into it, it means we commit the very act that is discouraged. However, it is entirely clear from this verse that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) made his wives his confidantes, which proves his complete trust in their discretion, understanding, and insight. The beauty of the relationship between husband and wife lies entirely in this mutual trust. If they lack the trust to share secrets, their life remains completely devoid of high human values.
(Proof of the cordial relations among the Mothers of the Believers): (and when she informed of it and Allah showed it to him, he made known part of it and avoided part) - Based on context, it is understood here that the wife whom the Prophet made his confidante disclosed this secret to another wife. Although the verse does not name the wife who committed this lapse or the wife to whom it was disclosed, it is clear that the matter remained entirely between the Mothers of the Believers; the secret was not disclosed to any outsider. Commentators generally name Aisha and Hafsah (may Allah be pleased with them). If this report is accepted, it clearly proves that the mutual relations among these noble ladies were so cordial that they shared important secrets with one another without any hesitation. This refutes the reports in which uncritical narrators depict constant jealousy and rivalry among them.
(In matters of divine limits, no one is above accountability): Allah Almighty informed the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) of this disclosure, after which the Prophet brought it to the attention of the wife who committed the lapse. This was necessary because confidentiality holds great importance in marital relations. Wives must protect their husbands' secrets. In the Qur'an, their distinct attribute is described as 'guardians of the unseen' (hafizatun lil-ghayb). A wife is the natural trustee of her husband's secrets; if she betrays them, it means the one appointed as the guardian of the house has breached its security. While this quality of trustworthiness is necessary for every wife, responsibilities vary according to status. The wives of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) bore a far greater responsibility than others. Therefore, at Allah's behest, the Prophet warned her so that this reality is clearly demonstrated to everyone: in matters of divine limits, no one is above accountability.
(A highly subtle reality): The words 'he made known part of it and avoided part' (arrafa ba'dahu wa a'rada 'an ba'd) point to a highly subtle reality. Specifically, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) held his wife accountable because the religion required it, but he did not do so in a harsh manner; rather, he mentioned a part of the matter and overlooked a part. The Prophet adopted this method so that while she received the warning, it would not weigh too heavily on her heart. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was inherently gentle and kind; even with the hypocrites and opponents, he never corrected their mistakes using harsh words. Furthermore, this involved the Mothers of the Believers, about whom it could not be assumed that any mistake was motivated by deliberate ill intent. Indeed, as noted above, this mistake was not the result of ill intent, but occurred simply because she thought that since the other wife was also beloved and trusted by the husband, there was no harm in sharing this private matter with her, and that it would rather increase mutual love and trust. This thought was based on good intentions, free of any malicious intent. However, she overlooked an important aspect—namely, that disclosing this secret was contrary to the conduct Allah and His Messenger preferred for wives, which was necessary to be found in its perfect form in the wives of the Prophet because they serve as models for all Muslim women. Moreover, this involved the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), who was not just a husband but also the Messenger of Allah. Due to the significance of the matter, she was corrected, but not in a way that caused public humiliation; rather, it was done through subtle indications. This is the most blessed approach when a mistake is committed without a bad intention.
(The indiscretion of commentators): We do not wish to engage in the commentators' attempts to unveil this secret. Their attempts are based on reports that contain clear contradictions. Furthermore, this prying is contrary to the very teaching of this verse: when the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself did not see fit to disclose it fully and Allah approved of his approach, how can it be permissible for anyone else to pry into it—especially when the matter is between the Messenger of Allah and his noble wives?
(And when he informed her of it, she said, 'Who informed you of this?' He said, 'I was informed by the Knowing, the Acquainted'): When the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) informed her of this through subtle indications, she immediately asked: Who informed you of this? She did not deny the truth of it, but instead of focusing on her lapse, she became concerned with how the Prophet had come to know. Her mind must have gone to the wife whom she had made her confidante, assuming she had reported it to the Prophet. There was context for this thought, as she had not disclosed the secret to anyone else. It is possible she felt some anger, as indicated by her question 'Who informed you?'. If so, it is not surprising; feeling hurt when one's trust is compromised is a natural human reaction. However, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) immediately cleared her misunderstanding, stating: I was informed by the All-Knowing and All-Acquainted God, not by any human. This explanation by the Prophet removed her suspicion, which was necessary to resolve, and therefore the Prophet deemed it necessary to clarify this without delay.

(If you two repent to Allah, [it is best], for your hearts have deviated [i.e., inclined]. But if you cooperate against him—then indeed, Allah is his protector, and Gabriel and the righteous of the believers and the angels, moreover, are [his] assistants) (4).
This addresses both wives, stating: if you turn back to Allah, it is most fitting for you, because your hearts are already inclined toward Allah. But if you cooperate against the Messenger, remember that he does not depend on you for his peace of mind; rather, Allah, Gabriel, and the righteous believers are sufficient as his companions, and the angels are also his helpers and assistants.
(A question and its answer): A question arises here: since the lapse was committed by only one wife, why is the address directed to two, and what mistake was committed by the second wife that she was also directed to repent, when she apparently seems innocent?
The answer is: when the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) expressed his displeasure with one wife for disclosing the secret, the second wife likely assumed that this displeasure was because the secret had been shared with her. She might have thought: 'The secret was shared only with me, not with an outsider, so why is there displeasure? This implies I am regarded as an outsider.' Although her feeling was based on a misunderstanding, such misunderstandings are not uncommon where mutual love and trust compete.
(Correction of misplaced pride): In any case, both noble ladies felt this correction was contrary to their pride, and this manifested in both wives temporarily withdrawing and showing some estrangement toward the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Under normal circumstances, this is not unusual; such minor friction occurs frequently between husbands and wives. However, this involved the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his noble wives, so Allah Almighty corrected them firmly. This was done to make it entirely clear to the wives of the Prophet that in matters of religion, no one has the right to display misplaced pride. Since they committed a lapse, they, more than anyone else, were expected to reform their attitude. This was what befitted them and was demanded by their faith and devotion. If they persisted in their attitude and cooperated against the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), they had to remember that the Messenger of Allah did not depend on them for his peace of mind; rather, they were the ones who depended on him.
It should be noted that the attitude shown by these wives was not motivated by hatred or anger, but rather—as clarified—by mutual love, trust, and a desire to be coaxed (tadallul). Yet, the Qur'an corrected them in firm words. The reason is what we pointed out in the introductory discussions: this Surah is primarily revealed to teach that even within the feelings of love, the limits and commands of Allah must be fully observed. This required the wives of the Prophet to regard him not merely as a husband, but under all circumstances as the Messenger of Allah, keeping this aspect foremost in all situations, as his status as a messenger is superior to all other relationships.
(If you two repent to Allah, [it is best], for your hearts have deviated [i.e., inclined]): Our commentators have committed a major error in interpreting this phrase. They took 'sagat' to mean 'deviated' or 'turned away' [in a bad sense] and interpreted it as: 'if you two repent, you must do so because your hearts have deviated from the truth.'
(Analysis of the word 'sagha'): This interpretation contains several errors, some of which we highlight:
1. First, this interpretation is entirely contrary to Arabic grammar. In Arabic, the word 'sagha' does not mean turning away or deviating from something; rather, it means inclining and leaning toward something. Our teacher explained the linguistic analysis of this word in his commentary on Surah At-Tahrim. We present the essential part of his explanation here. He writes:
'In all languages generally, and in Arabic specifically, certain words—while falling under a broad meaning—are reserved for highly specific nuances. Those unfamiliar with these linguistic characteristics remain entirely deprived of understanding them.' After stating this rule, he presents an example, writing:
'For example, "mayl" (inclining/turning) is a broad concept under which there are many words in Arabic, such as zaygh, jawr, righwa, hiyadah, and inhiraf. However, all of these are used for "mayl an al-shay" (turning away or deviating from something). Under the same broad concept, there are also words like tawbah, iltifaf, and sagha, all of which are used for "mayl ila al-shay" (inclining or leaning toward something)...'
'Once this linguistic reality is clear, it cannot remain hidden from a scholar of Arabic that "sagat qulubukuma" means "your hearts have turned in devotion and inclined toward Allah and His Messenger," because the word "saghu" is used for leaning toward something, not turning away from it.'
'This core meaning is present in all derivatives of the word. For example, "saghiyat al-rajul" refers to a person's devoted followers. "saghuha ma'aka" means his inclination is toward you. "asgaytu ila fulan" means you turned your ear toward him. In the Hadith, it is said: (The Trumpet will be blown, and no one will hear it except that he will incline his ear to it). Similarly, the proverb states: (The child knows best the bosom of love). In the Hadith regarding the cat, it is said: (He would tilt the vessel for her) so she could drink water easily. The inner curve of a vessel is called "saghu" because things accumulate in it.'
To prove the meaning of 'asgha' as listening attentively, Ibn Barri presented the following poetic verse:
'You see the fool turning away (zaygh) from every noble deed, while he inclines his ear (asgha') toward foolishness.'
Describing a camel, a poet says:
'She inclines her head (tasghi) and listens as he binds her saddle, and as soon as he steps into the stirrup, she leaps forward.'
Al-A'sha, describing his dog's eye, says:
'You see her eye inclined (saghra) toward the corner of her eyelid, watching my hand and the ready whip.'
Namir ibn Tawlab used the phrase 'asgha' inaa' (tilting the vessel) in a specific context, but the core meaning of the word is preserved:
'The vessel of a sister's son is tilted (meaning his rights are compromised) if he does not confront his maternal uncles with a brave father.'
After presenting these proverbs and verses, our teacher writes with deep conviction:
'These are the proofs for those who seek the truth. They will remain satisfied with them and will not be influenced by the distortions introduced into reports. When they found the path of literal distortion of the Divine Book closed, they opened paths of semantic distortion, interpreting "saghu" as "zaygh" (deviation), whereas there is a vast difference between the two. The variant reading "zagat" reported in some narrations is entirely unworthy of attention.'
(Explanation of an Arabic style of expression): 2. The second error is that if the intention was to say: 'you two must repent because your hearts have deviated,' then the style of expression adopted here by the Qur'an would be entirely inappropriate. The particle 'qad' appearing after the conditional 'in', as it does here, is discussed by our teacher, who presents several examples from the Qur'an and classical Arabic, writing:
'If you reflect on these examples, you will see that in this style, the sentence following "qad" explains the ease and naturalness of the action commanded after "in". If we unpack the ellipsis, the meaning is: if you do this, there is no harm or surprise, because this is already your established state. In this light, the verse is interpreted as: if you two repent to God to seek the pleasure of the Prophet, this is exactly what is expected of you, because your hearts are already inclined in devotion toward Him.'
(A reality of human psychology): 3. The third error is that it turns the Mothers of the Believers into sinners guilty of deviation and turning away from the truth without any basis. However, the nature of the incident we explained in the light of the Qur'anic text shows that there was no trace of malicious intent; rather, whatever happened occurred on the basis of mutual trust, love, and sincerity. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) shared a secret with one wife, who, out of love, shared it with another. When Allah informed the Prophet of this, he corrected her, but she did not give this correction its due weight, thinking that sharing the husband's secret with his other trusted and beloved wife was not a major error. When the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) appeared somewhat distant because of this, they—relying on the husband's love—withdrew in a coaxing manner, and the second wife supported her in this. As we noted, she likely felt somewhat hurt that a matter shared with her had become a cause of displeasure. It is not surprising for such feelings of self-respect to arise among noble ladies.
Keep in mind the psychological reality that when estrangement is caused by love and trust, the displeasure is merely an outward screen behind which lies a deep desire for reconciliation. This was the case here. Both wives appeared estranged outwardly, but every corner of their hearts was anxious for a gesture of affection from the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) so they could remove this artificial screen. However, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) could not soften his attitude because his family had to be taught to prioritize Allah's commands even within their love. Thus, the wives had to abandon their misplaced pride, but the bond of love is strong. Despite desiring to end this distance, they hesitated to take the first step. The Qur'an points beautifully to this inner conflict with the words 'for your hearts are already inclined', but unfortunately, our commentators failed to understand this and mistook this loving inclination of the heart for deviation.

(Perhaps his Lord, if he divorced you, would substitute for him wives better than you—submissive [to Allah], believing, devoutly obedient, repentant, worshipping, and fasting [or traveling]—previously married and virgins) (5).
(An exemplary mirror of noble qualities for the Prophet's wives): This continues the preceding theme, rendered more intense by the demands of correction. Addressing the Mothers of the Believers, it is stated: you should not assume that if you withdraw from the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), he will have no source of support. Allah can choose for him better wives than you who possess all the desirable qualities. The qualities listed here have been discussed in other places; refer to the commentary on Surah Al-Ahzab, verse 35, to avoid lengthy repetition.
The mention of 'previously married' (thayyibat) alongside 'virgins' (abkara) is to highlight that in the sight of Allah, true value lies in noble qualities; whether a woman is previously married or a virgin is a secondary matter. If noble qualities are present, a previously married woman holds precedence over a virgin; hence, 'previously married' is mentioned first here.
Commentators have generally translated 'sa'ihat' as 'those who fast', but in our view, this translation is incomplete. It is derived from 'siyahah', which is a religious term with a broad meaning. We have explained its complete analysis under Surah At-Tawbah, verse 112. Its essence is asceticism and turning away from the world; hence, it refers to those acts of worship and spiritual exercises that Islam prefers for asceticism, such as fasting, i'tikaf, and Hajj. It is essentially a form of monastic devotion. Just as monasticism is permitted only within a specific limit in Islam, 'siyahah' is also desired only within a specific limit. While fasting is a key element of this spiritual exercise, translating it simply as 'fasting' is not entirely accurate. I have translated it as 'devoted to spiritual exercises', which is comprehensive and covers all the acts of worship that fall under it, including fasting.
(The importance of accountability in religion): As you read these verses, keep in mind that this accountability involves the noble wives of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), whose purity is beyond doubt and who hold precedence over all women of the world. Yet, they were warned with the prospect of divorce simply because they showed a minor lapse in accepting the Prophet's training—and that too not out of hostility, but out of love and trust. This shows the status of accountability and training in religion. In an Islamic society, this is the very foundation of unity and separation. The love that is devoid of this accountability and training is not true love, but a trap of Satan. The beauty of the believers' love is that it remains subject to the limits of Allah. They are never unconcerned with one another in this regard; rather, they are most vigilant in correcting those whom they love most, for this is the requirement of true love. When they see that their sincere advice finds no place in the hearts, they do not hesitate to sever even the most beloved ties, because once the spiritual connection is lost, mere physical connection holds no value in their eyes.

(O you who have believed, protect yourselves and your families from a Fire whose fuel is people and stones, over which are [appointed] angels, harsh and severe; they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them but do what they are commanded) (6).
(General instruction for accountability): After correcting the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his noble wives, the general Muslims are addressed: make every effort to protect yourselves and your families from a Fire whose fuel is people and stones. The meaning is: when you have seen that even the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his wives are not above Allah's accountability, then what of others! Everyone must do everything they can to protect themselves and their families from the Fire. Whenever they see negligence toward the Shariah creeping into their families, they must immediately take steps to correct it. They should not worry that this correction might seem heavy or cause temporary displeasure. This temporary displeasure is far easier to bear than leaving them to head toward Hell and bearing the responsibility of guardianship before Allah on the Day of Judgment, as stated in the Hadith: (Each of you is a shepherd, and each of you will be questioned about his flock).
(The true fuel of Hellfire): (whose fuel is people and stones) - These words describe the nature of that Fire: its primary fuel will be people and stones. With this fuel, it will rage in its true form. 'People' refers to those who did not purify themselves in this world, but remained steeped in the impurities from which the Shariah was revealed to cleanse them.
'Stones' refers to the idols and stones that were worshipped in this world as symbols of shirk and disbelief, as explained under Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 24. This Fire was created to burn these very things, so when this fuel is delivered to it, it will, as it were, receive its desired food, and cry out: 'Are there any more?' devouring everything, as stated: (It leaves nothing and spares nothing), showing no mercy.
(The nature of the angels appointed over Hell): (over which are [appointed] angels, harsh and severe; they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them but do what they are commanded) - That is, the angels appointed over Hell will be extremely harsh and severe, showing no leniency or compromise to anyone. They will not deviate from any command they receive, but will execute it fully.
This is a rebuke to those who consider correcting their families for major errors as contrary to love. It is stated: if your love prevents you from correcting them today, remember that the angels appointed over Hell are not loving beings, but extremely harsh and severe. It is far better that before you confront them, you try to protect yourselves and your families from the punishment through your own accountability. (they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them but do what they are commanded) - That is, if the instruction to hold yourselves accountable seems heavy on your hearts today, let it be; it will not weigh heavily on the guardians of Hell. They will not violate any command of Allah, but will execute every order fully.

(O you who have disbelieved, make no excuses that Day. You will only be recompensed for what you used to do) (7).
That is, on that Day, there will be no room for anyone to make excuses. Those who wish to present excuses will be told: do not make excuses today. What you are facing is the harvest of what you sowed yourself. Suffer the consequences of the earnings you made in the world. These are not crimes committed by others that have been placed to your account; rather, the retribution for your own crimes is presenting itself before you, so your wailing is of no use. Whatever you could have done to save yourselves could only have been done in the world; now that time has passed.

(O you who have believed, repent to Allah with sincere repentance. Perhaps your Lord will remove from you your misdeeds and admit you into gardens beneath which rivers flow [on] the Day when Allah will not disgrace the Prophet and those who believed with him. Their light will proceed before them and on their right; they will say, 'Our Lord, perfect for us our light and forgive us. Indeed, You are over all things competent') (8).
(General invitation to repentance and turning to Allah): Following the preceding warning, all Muslims are invited to repent and turn to Allah: O you who have believed, abandon your bed of negligence and turn sincerely to your Lord. 'Sincere repentance' (tawbatan nasuha) refers to the repentance offered with complete devotion and true resolve, after which no desire remains to return to the sin; rather, the person bids final farewell to sin and submits entirely to his Lord. It is stated: those who repent in this manner can hope that Allah will remove the effects of their sins and admit them into gardens beneath which rivers flow. The word 'asa' (perhaps), when used by Allah to address His servants, carries the nature of a promise and glad tiding, provided the servants prove themselves worthy.
(on the Day when Allah will not disgrace the Prophet and those who believed with him): This 'Day' can be regarded as the adverb of time for the verb 'admit' (yudkhila), or an appropriate verb can be understood as omitted before it. It is stated: on that Day, Allah will not disgrace His Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his believing companions, because in this world, they held themselves accountable and selected for companionship only those who were entirely free from the stains of disbelief and hypocrisy. They will not be like those who led hypocritical lives and led their families toward hypocrisy through compromise. The hypocrites will be disgraced on the Day of Judgment because the darkness of their hypocrisy will surround them, while the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions (may Allah be pleased with them) will proceed toward their destination in the light of their faith and sincerity.
(The honor of the Prophet [peace and blessings of Allah be upon him] and his companions on the Day of Judgment): (Their light will proceed before them and on their right; they will say, 'Our Lord, perfect for us our light and forgive us. Indeed, You are over all things competent') - This describes the honor and success of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions. On that Day, while others wander in darkness, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions will have their light proceeding before them and on their right, guiding them, and they will pray: Our Lord, perfect our light for us and forgive us; indeed, You are powerful over all things.
This light was also mentioned in Surah Al-Hadid, where those who will be disgraced on that Day are described. He says:
(On the Day you see the believing men and believing women, their light proceeding before them and on their right, [it will be said], 'Your glad tidings today are [of] gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein you will abide eternally.' That is the great attainment. On the [same] Day the hypocritical men and hypocritical women will say to those who believed, 'Wait for us that we may acquire some of your light.' It will be said, 'Go back behind yourselves and seek light.' And a wall will be placed between them with a door, its interior containing mercy, but on the outside of it is punishment. The hypocrites will call to them, 'Were we not with you?' They will say, 'Yes, but you led yourselves into temptation and awaited [disaster] and doubted, and wishful thinking deluded you until there came the command of Allah. And the Deceiver deluded you concerning Allah.') (Al-Hadid, 57:12-14).
(Our Lord, perfect for us our light and forgive us. Indeed, You are over all things competent): This light will be shown to them before entering Paradise to guide them along the path when pitch darkness surrounds them everywhere else. Therefore, they will pray with deep gratitude: O Lord, perfect this light for us and forgive us. Obviously, when this light is granted to guide them along the path, it will naturally inspire the hope that Allah is going to bless them with His forgiveness, and that moment will mark the perfect manifestation of this light; hence, they will pray for the fulfillment of this hope.
(Indeed, You are over all things competent): This will be an expression of the reality they held as a matter of faith in the world, stating: we believed that You are powerful over all things, and now we have witnessed this reality with our own eyes. Unquestionably, You are powerful over all things.

(O Prophet, strive against the disbelievers and the hypocrites and be harsh upon them. And their refuge is Hell, and wretched is the destination) (9).
After directing the accountability of the believers and their families, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is commanded with the general duty of striving against the opponents. The 'jihad' commanded in the verse involves both the sword and the tongue, each in its appropriate place. Against those who actively opposed the truth after the proof had been established, the struggle was with the sword—as detailed in the previous Surahs. Against those who outwardly claimed faith but avoided its obligations, the instruction was to hold them accountable, and to do so not in a gentle manner, but with harshness (and be harsh upon them).
(Instruction to adopt a harsh approach in holding the hypocrites accountable): This instruction was given because the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), out of his noble nature, would always correct their mistakes gently to avoid humiliating them. They should have appreciated this noble approach, but the hypocrites were not worthy of it. Instead of benefiting from it, they grew bolder, assuming their deception was succeeding. Therefore, Allah Almighty commanded the Prophet to change this approach and hold them accountable with harshness, so they might take heed and reform themselves, leaving them with no excuse. This theme has been explained under Surah At-Tawbah, verse 73; refer to it for details.
(And their refuge is Hell, and wretched is the destination): That is, inform them clearly that if they do not change their way, their destination is Hell, and it is a wretched destination.

(Allah presents an example of those who disbelieved: the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot. They were under two of our righteous servants but betrayed them, so those prophets did not avail them against Allah at all, and it was said, 'Enter the Fire with those who enter') (10).
(What avails a person is their own deeds, not their lineage or relationships): Finally, an example is presented to show that what avails a person before Allah is their own righteous deeds. One who lacks the treasure of righteous deeds cannot benefit from any relationship with the most noble of creation. It is stated: the wives of Noah (peace be upon him) and Lot (peace be upon him) were married to two of Our righteous servants, but instead of following their excellent pattern, they betrayed them. As a result, despite being the wives of messengers, they were told: enter the Fire along with those who enter.
This example is presented for the general instruction of the disbelievers, as stated: (Allah presents an example of those who disbelieved). Its connection to the incidents of the Mothers of the Believers mentioned at the beginning of the Surah is only of a general, principled nature; it is incorrect to assume it was revealed specifically in relation to them. The greatest neglect of accountability in the Hereafter, especially among religious people, has been caused by the delusion that because they are the descendants of God's beloved and chosen servants, the Fire of Hell will never touch them. The Jews and Christians were ruined by this very trial, and now many Muslims suffer from the same delusion.
In the verse, the word 'khanata' (they betrayed) is used for both women, which clearly shows that they were not faithful to their husbands' secrets and mission. However, this does not imply any moral impropriety of a sexual nature; Allah Almighty protects His prophets from any such blemish. Narrations state that the wife of Lot (peace be upon him) would inform the wicked men of her nation of the guests who came to him. This kind of betrayal is described here as 'khiyanah' (betrayal).

(And Allah presents an example of those who believed: the wife of Pharaoh, when she said, 'My Lord, build for me near You a house in Paradise and save me from Pharaoh and his deeds and save me from the wrongdoing people') (11).
(It is obligatory to protect one's faith even in the worst environment): This example is presented for the instruction of the believers, showing that a person is obligated to protect their faith even in the most corrupt environment. Even if that environment offers luxury, their faith requires them to perceive it as a source of torment, and even if it becomes a place of suffering for them, they must protect their faith by enduring all hardships. It is stated: the wife of Pharaoh was a queen living in a palace, yet her prayer was: O my Lord, build for me a house near You in Paradise, and save me from Pharaoh, his deeds, and the wrongdoing people. This shows that she perceived her environment as a place of torment because it was filled with tyranny and disobedience. A believer should never feel content in an environment of disbelief, disobedience, and tyranny, even if they personally possess a royal palace within it.
(save me from Pharaoh and his deeds and save me from the wrongdoing people): This also indicates that she was well aware that if a person remains content and pleased in a corrupt environment, even if they are not directly involved in its evils, they cannot remain secure from its consequences. To remain secure, it is necessary to loathe it and flee from it whenever the opportunity arises.

(And Mary, the daughter of 'Imran, who guarded her chastity, so We blew into [her garment] through Our angel, and she believed in the words of her Lord and His scriptures and was of the devoutly obedient) (12).
(An exemplary model of noble upbringing in a corrupt environment): Finally, the example of Mary (peace be upon her) is presented. Although she was raised in a corrupt environment, she achieved a highly distinguished status with Allah through her personal devotion, effort, spiritual exercise, and constant worship.
During the period she was born, the Children of Israel were subjects of the Romans, and their moral and religious decay had reached the level indicated by the severe rebukes they received from Prophet John (peace be upon him) and later from Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him). Their character is also evident from their hostile attitude toward John, Jesus, and other righteous servants of the time, which ultimately resulted in their being cursed by Allah. In this darkest period, Mary (peace be upon her) was born, without any prominent worldly support. Yet, from her childhood, when she was in devotion in the Temple, Prophet Zechariah (peace be upon him) (who was her maternal uncle) would visit her and be amazed by her spiritual excellence. Inspired by her virtues, he prayed for a similarly righteous child for himself, which was granted through the birth of Prophet John (peace be upon him).
Mary (peace be upon her) is an eternal example showing that if a person possesses true devotion, they can make themselves an object of envy even for the angels within the worst of environments. Consequently, Allah found her worthy to bear His great trust, and to protect her honor from the slanders of the wicked, He manifested His power in a manner never shown for anyone else under the heavens.
(who guarded her chastity, so We blew into through Our angel): This points to her core virtue through which she became worthy of bearing Allah's great trust. The word 'farj' in Arabic does not carry a narrow meaning; its literal meaning is 'a place of fear or vulnerability'. All the avenues through which evil can enter a person are included in its meaning. In the Qur'an, this term is used for both men and women. 'ahsanat farjaha' means she kept a strict guard over all parts of her existence where any evil could find a way. As a reward for this, Allah blew His spirit into her, and His great sign was manifested from her womb in the form of Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him).
(and she believed in the words of her Lord and His scriptures and was of the devoutly obedient): Following her specific virtue, this points to her general virtues of faith: she accepted and executed every command she received from her Lord without hesitation, and stood firm upon every teaching of His scriptures. She was constantly mindful of her Lord, which enabled her to execute His commands and maintain her composure even during the most difficult trials.
(A point worthy of special attention): It is highly worthy of attention that for the example of disbelief, women were selected, and for the example of belief, women were also named. This is intended to dispel the common misconception that woman is the source of all evil. By nature, a woman possesses the potential for both good and evil. If she does not use her choice and will correctly, she can become the worst companion to the best of men; but if she tastes the sweetness of faith and devotion, she remains a resident of Paradise even in the most corrupt environment. By the grace of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed. All praise belongs to Allah for His favor and kindness. Rahmanabad, June 6, 1978 / Jumada al-Thani 28, 1398 AH.
